title: Cherry scones
subtitle: Glacé cherry-studded sweet scones
excerpt: Plain scones with glacé cherries folded through. The cherries soften in the bake and the sugar in the syrup colours the crumb a faint pink. Twelve scones from a quick mix and a short bake.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): cherry scones appear in the tea-table chapter as a children's bake. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the candied-fruit handling for tea-table cakes (rinse and dry chopped cherries before folding into the dough) is laid out in the household-baking chapter.

recipe.freezeNotes: Open-freeze unbaked scones on a tray; bag once solid; bake from frozen on a hot tray, adding 3 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 24 across two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Chop and rinse the cherries the day before to drive off the syrup; chill the dry mix.

glossaryTerms.definition: Glacé cherries are preserved in heavy sugar syrup; if added straight to a dough they sink and weep colour. Tip the cherries into a sieve, rinse under warm water for ten seconds, and dry on kitchen paper. The fruit then folds in cleanly and holds its colour.

content.text: A small modification on the plain scone: rinsed glacé cherries are folded through the rubbed-in flour before the milk goes in. The bake is otherwise the same. The cherries stay whole in the bake and soften the crumb where they touch.
Serve warm with butter and a little jam. The cherries are sweet enough that the scones do not need cream as well, though there is no harm in it.
Prepare the cherries and rub in the butter
Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a baking tray. Tip the {{glace-cherries}} into a sieve and

for ten seconds; dry on kitchen paper. Sift the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a bowl. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}} and the cherries.

Pour in the {{whole-milk}} all at once. Stir with a palette knife in figure-of-eight strokes until the dough just comes together. Tip onto a floured surface and pat to a 3 cm slab. Cut twelve rounds with a floured 5 cm cutter, pressing straight down. Re-pat the trimmings once; the second-round scones rise less so set them at the back of the tray.
Brush the tops with the beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 10–12 minutes; the tops should be deep gold and the sides risen and dry. Cool on a rack for at least 5 minutes before splitting.
